text: The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector in the left hand and a curved needle driver in the right hand to perform suturing of the gastrojejunal anastomosis at increased speed. Two ovicral sutures are used, which will dissolve completely in approximately seventy days.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector in the left hand and a curved needle driver in the right hand to perform suturing of the gastrojejunal anastomosis at increased speed. Two ovicral sutures are used, which will dissolve completely in approximately seventy days.

The surgeon uses dyed and undyed sutures to construct the gastrojejunal anastomosis, starting at the tip of the rural limb and suturing it to the lateral corner of the gastric pouch. The posterior outer layer of the anastomosis is being sutured, with bites taken slightly beyond the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum and incorporating the staple line from the transverse staple fire on the gastric pouch.

The surgeon uses dyed and undyed sutures to construct the gastrojejunal anastomosis, starting at the tip of the rural limb and suturing it to the lateral corner of the gastric pouch. The posterior outer layer of the anastomosis is sutured, with bites taken slightly beyond the anti-mesenteric border of the jejunum and incorporating the staple line from the transverse staple fire on the gastric pouch.
The surgeon locks the final suture on the medial border of the gastrojejunal anastomosis, completing the hand-sewn technique.

The surgeon locks the final suture on the medial border of the gastrojejunal anastomosis. An assistant introduces the laparoscopic section while the anesthesiologist advances a 34 French sizing tube. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the gastric pouch to match the sizing tube, employing a disposable gastric lavage tube to avoid damaging a reusable bougie. The harmonic scalpel is then used to open the jejunum, with caution advised due to the small bowel's tendency to dilate more than the stomach.

The assistant introduces the laparoscopic section while the anesthesiologist advances a 34 French sizing tube. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully open the gastric pouch to match the size of the sizing tube. A disposable gastric lavage tube is used to prevent damage to a reusable bougie. The harmonic scalpel is then used to create an enterotomy in the jejunum, with caution due to the tendency of the small bowel to dilate more than the stomach.

The surgeon constructs the posterior inner layer of the gastrojejunal anastomosis, starting with a forehand stitch on the stomach and using a backhanded stitch for the jejunum. The laparoscopic suturing technique is visibly emphasized.

The surgeon constructs the posterior inner layer of the gastrojejunal anastomosis using a forehand stitch on the stomach, followed by a backhanded stitch on the jejunum. The assistant retracts the jejunum with a suction device to facilitate the suturing process. Undyed sutures are used for the inner layers to differentiate them from the dyed outer layer sutures.

The surgeon takes the first bite of the posterior inner layer through the stomach, bringing the suture inside the gastric pouch. The assistant retracts the jejunum using a suction device, facilitating forehand suturing of the posterior inner layer. Undyed sutures are used for the inner layers to differentiate them from the dyed outer layer sutures. Forehand suturing progresses until single bites of the jejunum and stomach are required.

The surgeon continues forehand through-and-through suturing of the gastrojejunal anastomosis until single bites of the jejunum and stomach are required. Backhanded sutures are used in difficult corners. The posterior inner layer suture is left untied to be connected with the anterior inner layer suture, which begins on the jejunal side and is tied to itself.

The surgeon uses a backhanded suture technique to navigate the difficult corners of the anastomosis. The posterior inner layer suture is left untied, while the anterior inner layer suture is initiated on the jejunal side and tied to itself, excluding the posterior inner layer's starting suture.

The surgeon begins running the anterior inner layer using a forehand technique, with the needle positioned at a forward tilt, slightly facing away from the surgeon and the needle driver, facilitating a through and through suturing technique.

The surgeon begins running the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis using a forehand technique. The needle is positioned with a forward tilt, slightly facing away from the surgeon and the needle driver, facilitating a through-and-through suturing approach.

The surgeon checks the position of the final suture from the posterior inner layer while approaching it on the anterior inner layer. The enterotomy is observed to be sufficiently closed, and a seromuscular suture may be applied for the final stitch.

The surgeon checks the position of the final suture from the posterior inner layer while approaching it on the anterior inner layer. Once the enterotomy is sufficiently closed, a seromuscular suture is applied for the final stitch.
The surgeon refrains from tying the sutures at this stage, opting to wait until the sizing tube is passed through the anastomosis to determine its size.

The surgeon introduces a dyed suture for the anterior outer layer, using other sutures as a handle to place the initial stitch. The tying of sutures is delayed until after the sizing tube is passed to size the anastomosis.

The surgeon introduces a dyed suture for the anterior outer layer, using existing sutures as a handle to place the initial stitch at the tip of the roux limb. The surgeon then cuts the other sutures to prevent interference with the remaining suturing and tying, proceeding with a forehand technique.

The surgeon cuts the sutures to prevent interference with the remaining suturing and tying. They begin suturing at the tip of the roux limb using a forehand technique. The sizing tube has not yet been passed, allowing for easier placement of the far lateral sutures in the anterior outer layer before the tube is introduced.
The surgeon places the initial sutures into the anterior outer layer before passing the sizing tube, noting the increased difficulty of performing far lateral sutures once the tube is in place.

The assistant braces the lateral side of the anastomosis while the surgeon's left hand is positioned posteriorly. The needle driver is used on the medial side as the anesthesiologist applies pressure to pass the sizing tube through the anastomosis. Once the tube is inside, an inner layer suture is cut, and the needle is removed from the abdomen. The inner layer sutures are then tied together, and the needle is removed again.

The assistant braces the lateral side of the anastomosis while the surgeon's left hand is positioned posteriorly. The needle driver is used on the medial side as the anesthesiologist applies pressure to pass the sizing tube through the anastomosis. Once inside, a suture from the inner layer is cut, and the needle is removed from the abdomen. The inner layer sutures are then tied together, and the needle is removed again. A backhand suture from the medial side of the posterior outer layer is used to maneuver the suture out of the corner.

The surgeon uses a backhand suture technique from the medial side to work the suture out of the corner of the posterior outer layer. The anterior outer layer sutures are completed in a seromuscular fashion, folding the jejunum over the anterior inner layer of sutures. Upon reaching the corner, the sutures are tightened, and one needle is removed from the abdomen.

The surgeon completes the anterior outer layer sutures in a seromuscular fashion, folding the jejunum over the anterior inner layer of sutures. Upon reaching the corner, the sutures are tightened, and one needle is removed from the abdomen. The anterior and posterior outer layer sutures are then tied together.

The surgeon ties the anterior and posterior outer layer sutures together, ensuring the anastomosis is secure. Multiple needles are visible in the abdomen, which are safely removed after use. The thirty-four French sizing tube is extracted, followed by a leak test to verify the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon removes the thirty-four French sizing tube from the anastomosis and performs a leak test to ensure the integrity of the sutured area.
